sources.name: TASVideos Game Resources — NES / Ninja Gaiden
sources.notes: Primary source. Documents all horizontal speeds, jump velocity lookup tables, wall jump tables, damage boost tables, and sub-pixel mechanics.

notes: Ninja Gaiden uses a VELOCITY LOOKUP TABLE for jump arcs — similar to Castlevania, NOT a physics simulation.
Jump velocity is read from a predefined table each frame rather than computed from a gravity constant.
This means jump arc behavior is deterministic and exactly repeatable frame-by-frame.
Ryu has THREE distinct horizontal speeds (1.5, 1.0, 0.5) depending on context — no single 'run speed.'
Wall jump and wall cling are core mechanics — unique among NES action platformers of this era.
Sub-pixel system uses dedicated X and Y sub-pixel registers (fractional position tracking).

subpixel_system.description: Dedicated sub-pixel registers for both X and Y axes. Exact sub-pixels-per-pixel unit needs ROM verification.
subpixel_system.notes: TASVideos documents sub-pixel manipulation techniques, confirming fractional position tracking exists.

horizontal_movement._note: Ryu has THREE context-dependent horizontal speeds — not a single walk/run speed.

forward_speed.context: Running on ground OR pressing forward direction while airborne

damage_knockback_speed.context: After taking damage — Ryu bounces at this speed until landing on a platform or wall
damage_knockback_speed.notes: Damage knockback moves at 1.0 px/f. Ends when Ryu contacts a wall or platform (not mid-air).

backward_air_speed.notes: Pressing against travel direction in air reduces speed to 0.5 px/f. This allows precise air positioning at the cost of horizontal speed.

jump._note: Jump arc is a LOOKUP TABLE, not a physics simulation. Each frame reads a velocity value from a table rather than applying a gravity constant.

notes: Velocity table: 5 frames at -4, 5 frames at -3, 4 frames at -2, 4 frames at -1, 6 frames at 0 (peak), then positive (falling).
Total rise 48 pixels = 3 tiles.
Peak is a 6-frame window of 0 velocity — wider peak than many NES games.
Unlike Mario or Mega Man, this is not variable height — arc is committed at jump press.
Jump height is FIXED — holding jump does NOT extend height (like Castlevania, unlike Mario).
Full horizontal air control — direction can be changed mid-jump.
The combination (fixed height, free direction) is the same unusual combination as Contra.

wall_jump._note: Wall jump/cling is a CORE mechanic in Ninja Gaiden — distinguishing it from most NES platformers.

notes: Wall jump is significantly weaker than standing jump: 15px vs 48px rise.
5 frames at -2, 5 frames at -1, 5 frames at 0 (peak), then falling.
Wall jumps can be chained: jump to wall → wall jump → jump to next wall → wall jump.
Chaining wall jumps allows ascending vertical shafts.

wall_cling.description: Ryu can grab and hang from walls when airborne and pressing toward a wall.
notes: Wall cling halts downward momentum — Ryu hangs stationary.
From cling: press jump to wall jump away.
Wall cling is how players reset their horizontal direction before a wall jump.
This mechanic is unusually advanced for a 1988 NES game — predates Super Mario World wall jump by 2 years.

damage_boost._note: Taking damage produces a bounce trajectory — also lookup-table based.
notes: Damage bounce: 5 frames at -3, 5 at -2, 4 at -1, 6 at 0 (peak), then fall.
Horizontal speed during damage bounce: 1.0 px/f (the middle speed tier).
Damage boost can be exploited for reaching otherwise inaccessible platforms (damage boost glitch).
Bounce continues at 1.0 px/f until Ryu contacts a surface.

ladder.notes: 1 px/frame vertical on ladders. Can attack from ladders.

dragon_sword.notes: Standard melee attack. Short horizontal reach. High damage per hit.

weapons.notes: Ninjutsu arts use a resource (scroll) system. Different arts serve different tactical needs.

ryu_standing.notes: Approximate. Ryu's hitbox is narrower than his sprite. VERIFY against ROM.

design_notes_for_giants_drink: The lookup table jump arc (like Castlevania) commits the player to a fixed trajectory. Unlike Castlevania, Ninja Gaiden allows full horizontal air control — so the skill expression is in when and where you jump, not in how long you hold.

The wall cling + wall jump system creates vertical movement opportunities not found in most NES platformers of the era. Level design can use tall shafts and vertical traversal as a first-class challenge type.

The three horizontal speeds (1.5 forward, 1.0 knockback, 0.5 backward-in-air) create a tactile speed vocabulary. Players quickly learn to maintain forward momentum and feel the cost of retreating in air.
The 48px standing jump vs 15px wall jump creates a strong hierarchy of jump types. Wall jumps are escape tools, not primary traversal — level design should use this distinction.

Ninja Gaiden's cinematic cutscenes (unusual for the era) established a narrative-first action game tradition. Giants Drink's social ecology system can draw from this: action + character development + story progression are not mutually exclusive.
